title: Fixing Inconsistent Child Node Count in a Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Given a binary tree with parent-child relationships, write a function to check if every node has either one or no child node. The function should return True if all nodes have valid child counts, False otherwise.
statement.inputFormat: A binary tree represented as a list of tuples, where each tuple contains a node's value and its child node values.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value indicating whether all nodes have valid child counts.
statement.notes: Time complexity should be O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree.

examples.explanation: All nodes have either one or no child node.

hints.text: Use a recursive depth-first search to traverse the tree and count child nodes.

editorial.title: Correcting Child Node Counts in a Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to recursively traverse the tree and count child nodes.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible solution is to use a brute-force approach and manually count child nodes for each node.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a recursive approach with a depth-first search to traverse the tree and count child nodes.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The optimal solution ensures that all nodes are visited and child nodes are counted correctly.
editorial.dryRun: Start with a sample input and work through the recursive traversal to see how child nodes are counted.
commonMistakes: Failing to account for leaf nodes with no child nodes.
Incorrectly counting child nodes in the recursive traversal.
